title: Making Clinical & Ethical Decisions as an Injector. What You Need To Know As A New Injector
filename: Making Clinical & Ethical Decisions as an Injector. What You Need To Know As A New Injector.en.vtt
excerpt: Um so I gather you've got some clinical decisions as well that you want to talk about um let's dive into those is there an age limit which you won't treat with Botox and obviously legally it's 18 but is there a lower age limit well you'll say you're under 25 you I don't want to b…

segments.text: um so I gather you've got some clinical decisions as well that you want to talk about um let's dive into those is there an age limit which you won't treat with Botox and obviously legally it's 18 but is there a lower age limit well you'll say you're under 25 you I don't want to be treating you at this age because with risk resistance the botox potency like lessons with time is there like a lower age limit that you won't treat so I think that age is one factor that you might take into account the true way that a proper medical decision is made is that it's based wholly around the individual that you're in front of so I don't like anything that's a blanket decision and you know I've never treated someone less than 18 for example even before it was illegal but I actually think if they'd regulated this around medical practitioners that law would not be needed because the medical way of decision making is that you would have to justify that intervention that is in the best interest of the patient so I in my GP practice I can prescribe the pill to a 12 year old like it doesn't get that more controversial than

that but I can't treat an 18 year old about uh 17 and a half year old with Botox and I think although I've never had this indication there probably are some people who you could argue would have a really good benefit from it you know like maybe Master reduction so they've actually got something that's actually different you know that's causing them frustration and drama at school they might be getting bullied over something if it's dramatic enough you could argue that it was in their interest to change it but now I no longer have that option as a doctor and that is only because that was an easier thing to do than to regulate the whole industry to make it medical because a medical doctor does all sorts of things that are for the right patient wrong for everyone else if you know what I mean but it could be right for that patient so I try and think that way around it the numbers to know are older than 65 you're off label so all the data's around patients that are younger than that I'm younger than 18 we can't do anyway and the typical 21 year old probably doesn't need it like there's nothing distracting but you there are people who have hyperdynamic

muscles that are that they've been I've seen I have been asked for a treatment by 15 year old in a salon who had four headlines horizontal forehead lines so she clearly had lines on her forehead that I didn't have at that age even without Botox I had not developed You could argue that there was that would be the kind of case that might be in certain circumstance is worth it but the point is although she was never treated you'll have the same when she's 21 and I wouldn't have a rule I look at each patient and say do they have lines at rest so I'm not a big fan of using Botox as a preventative so I've had this sort of term thrown about that's just a marketing term like it's a what we call a communication Innovation you communicate the same thing in a different way so let's just put Less in and give it a new brand and then people who want less will have it and then most of them actually end up having more over time you're designing around the individual you'll meet as an 18 year old with deep lines one day who needs a full dose treatment and if you just blank it and know for everyone that could cause you an issue one of the reasons you might do it though is

because if you think there's a reputational risk so because we're not purely Public Health if you think that in your neighborhood if there are like for some reason lots of 18 year olds who seem to have deep frown lines and you've treated them all and everyone's like they don't see the before they only see the after and they don't understand it then you could make the argument that you'd rather not but that's the only reason I could think of not doing it other than just treating the patient in front of you yeah take every patient every patient's story yeah and take into account they're like holistic health yeah how it's affecting them I can't stop frowning everyone thinks I'm pissed off but I'm not yeah I've got arrested on this yeah okay when you can have a treatment uh I'm 12 I'm 25 I've got no lines at all but I just want to stop any lines developing I don't think you should have a treatment because you might do that for 10 years before you get a line you'd rather put into your mortgage you know yeah it's always someone because you can just you people some people get into their head that they have to do this or their face is going to fall apart and that's not true you can wait for the lines to

develop and if they're any little they'll disappear in two weeks and patience will appreciate you being honest won't they yeah and that goes across just to encourage us even more I've got so many patients by saying a flat no to people who then Feel so amazed that someone actually didn't just treat them that they actually refer patients to you yeah it's not just money grabbing are you yeah it's a long-term grain yeah just another question with botox so if I'm using the normal Botox brand I never I'm getting great results for patients over time do you change to a different brand to like prevent the resistance of that ever happening or if you're getting good results do you just keep going I would normally just continue I wouldn't switch it kind of complicates things um resistance I don't think it's that well understood they say the incidence is very low so you it doesn't actually happen as often as people fear but it definitely happens and I've had people who think it's very low disagreeing with me I'm like I literally have like four patients who have been through every brand of Botox with and none of them work and they cry like it's not like I'm not trying and yes you can put the dose up a bit

but there comes a point where you're using so much that you've got to explain that somehow and they never used to use so much so then you have to leave a much longer space or do other treatments it's definitely a thing I think it's more likely in people who have much more regular follow-ups so there are certain people come for every follow-up they always come for additional treatment um they come you know at as soon as they can and they try harder and eventually it basically stops do you book them patients in for because what I'm doing at the moment is I'm booking patients back in for the follow-up do you manually book them or like At first did you or because I've sort of booked them in because I want them before and after photos but once I've got a lot of them should I be booking them in myself should I let them come back I don't know so um in the beginning I think it's a really good idea for you to see everyone back because you get a ton of learning you get to get them into the habit of seeing you will you can see what individual injections especially if you take pictures with the marketing and then you see what you've done that will speed up your learning faster than anything else that you can do so it's really worth it in the long run it

can become not worth it because you will be training them to come back multiple times you tend to do it that'll adjust them to top up don't they yeah and unless you iron are very focused in making sure that it's just a check and that you don't need to have an injection and we've done the right job um you can end up in a dynamic where you're spending a lot of time and money on a lot of patients so don't really need to be seen I would do it for a while and then try and switch away from that and train our typical follow-up rate was about 15 to give you an idea so 15 people would have an adjustment and it could probably be lower than that yeah I think just once you get confident with your injection patterns and you know that they shouldn't need a review then maybe don't manually and if they've come back you've got a picture of the original treatment and then you do a Top-Up you then know what you can do on the next treatment safely and once they understand that they should understand why they don't need to come back as well but in the beginning it's a good sitting back and ask them that question of how has that affected you how's life been since so um because then you can better understand how these treatments actually affect not just the lines but actually the whole of the person yeah another

question is so windowing creams I just use the LMX and I've seen some of these crazy numbing creams going around are they they're quite unsafe aren't they so there's an issue with lidocaine toxicity so if you do that if you do the maths on it in terms of how much can be harmful for you A lot of them can tip into that quite easily so um obviously it's very tempting because the theory is oh we'll just completely stop all the pain with this yeah and I feel bad for clients so I feel like if I could help them in any way to get a higher numbing cream but then I've heard that it's dangerous yeah I think you can especially with this sort of 50 stuff if you haven't got the master with me now but if you work out percentage-wise how strong it is and how much it would take it's actually not that much if they were to get it into their mouth before they get some symptoms from it it's not insanely dangerous like otherwise you wouldn't I don't think it would exist at all but it's definitely on that spectrum of if you go a little bit too hard on it then don't risk it cardiac problems and various other and it does happen I think four percent replied applied enough time you know while you're doing other things give it time to work yeah and if you do

an injections to the Bone like on the cheekbones how much difference does numb and cream really make so it makes a difference to the entry point because that's and that's where most of the sensation is um otherwise if you're where the nerves are on the bone you don't get much benefit at all so the best example of that is probably a nasolabial fold or your info orbital nerve will come down and Supply the top lip so if you do that on the periosteum injection there they often feel that and then they also get numbing result as you inject The Villa with lidocaine in there the top lip goes numb cheeks um you might hit a nerve occasionally but it's less likely to and so most of the benefit is probably with the skin just a little first bit of injection yeah but you're right it doesn't help much underneath the layer of layers of the skin maybe it's a bit mild enough Placebo that the patient might feel better because they've got the wind cream on so that definitely helps if that helps them then here's a cheeky tip very anxious patient who's having multiple areas treated but not having their lips done I will often when I'm putting the cream on just skim the corner of their lip and it's it works like magic they go oh that cream really

works doesn't it yeah and like yes it does really work yeah just get it into the head that it works yeah and that just puts them in a frame of mind of thinking it's not going to be bad because it's mainly psychological like a lot of these the pain with injections I'm not saying all of it but for me as a person being injected I used to have a bit of a needle phobia and I remember the first time I had bozok's Dr Saba treated me she did not enjoy it because I was very anxious issued but it was all in my head because once I'd done it once like it's nothing really yeah it's fine but just as the psychological part of it in there yeah [Music]
